ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Bhuna Mutton; mapped to generic mutton dish class (FOODON_03302051) as closest defensible parent anchor. Bhuna preparation specifics are not separately coded in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked sheep/mutton; Bhuna preparation (dry-frying with spices, tomatoes, onions) is not separately coded. Used as closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for Bhuna Mutton as of 2026.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander powder, chilli powder, garam masala, turmeric)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Bhuna Mutton is approximately 150g, equivalent to about 4–5 boneless pieces of mutton with gravy, as commonly served as a side dish with rice or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Bhuna Mutton — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bhuna Mutton (cr_id G00010847). All per-100g core values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 5.5g/100g based on typical mutton fat composition (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 17174, cooked lamb shoulder) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bhuna Mutton. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with low carbohydrate content (carbohydrate 7.44g/100g, predominantly from tomatoes and onions). Meat-dominant dishes with minimal starch consistently fall in the low GI range (GI < 40) per University of Sydney GI Database category guidance. Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as: (30 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.35, rounded to 3.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100g or less, request reduced salt in preparation, and pair with potassium-rich low-sodium sides such as leafy greens to partially offset the sodium load; the tomato-based gravy contributes some potassium (210mg/100g) but is insufficient to compensate for the high sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.44g/100g) and high protein (14.05g/100g) support glycaemic stability, but high fat (11.58g/100g) may contribute to insulin resistance with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bhuna Mutton is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (100–150g) due to its low carbohydrate and high protein content; however, the high fat content (11.58g/100g, saturated fat approximately 5.5g/100g) warrants pairing with a high-fibre side such as salad or whole grain roti to improve overall glycaemic response and limit saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 5.5g/100g and cholesterol 85mg/100g from mutton, combined with sodium 467mg/100g, present compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Bhuna Mutton infrequently and in small portions (≤100g), request reduced oil in preparation to lower saturated fat, and avoid adding salt; the high sodium and saturated fat content together elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk, and substituting mutton with lean chicken or fish is advisable for regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6); tomatoes and chilli are nightshade ingredients; saturated fat approximately 5.5g/100g exceeds the 5g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Bhuna Mutton as mutton is a primary RA disqualifier due to its arachidonic acid content, nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, chilli) may trigger joint inflammation, and the high saturated fat and sodium content further worsen RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhuna Mutton (also known as Bhuna Gosht) is a popular Indian meat dish consisting of boneless pieces of mutton (lamb) cooked using the 'bhuna' technique — a method of dry-frying spices and meat together over high heat until the moisture evaporates and the spices coat the meat in a thick, intensely flavoured paste. The dish is prepared with fresh tomatoes, onions, bell pepper, garlic, ginger, coriander, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander powder, chilli powder, garam masala, and turmeric. The result is a semi-dry, richly spiced mutton dish with a deep reddish-brown colour, tender meat, and a concentrated, aromatic gravy. In Singapore, Bhuna Mutton is commonly served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as part of a rice or roti meal, particularly at lunch and dinner. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05g protein, 11.58g fat (saturated fat approximately 5.5g), 7.44g carbohydrate, 4.69g sugar, 0.3g dietary fibre, and 467mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance. The high sodium content (467mg/100g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension without strict portion control. The saturated fat and cholesterol (85mg/100g) are concerns for cardiovascular health. The low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (estimated 30) make it glycaemically favourable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions. The dish is halal and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: Is Bhuna Mutton suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in Bhuna Mutton?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010847, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb data as HPB does not separately report this field. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database category guidance for low-carbohydrate meat dishes. All other values are exact from the HPB canonical record.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this specific Bhuna Mutton preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010847) and is not available for an equivalent composite dish in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: full vitamin panel for this composite Bhuna Mutton dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010847) nor confirmed in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for an equivalent preparation as of 2026. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010847) for this dish, and no equivalent composite dish entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy confirms these values as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite cooked dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010847) as of 2026. saturated_fat_g estimated at 5.5g/100g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17174 (cooked lamb shoulder) as the closest approved-source analogue; documented as an adapted value. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate for a low-carbohydrate meat-based curry; no direct GI measurement for Bhuna Mutton exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (30 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.35, rounded to 3.3. The input record contained a minor spelling error ('bell peper') which has been corrected to 'bell pepper'. All core macros (protein_g 14.05, fat_g 11.58, carbohydrate_g 7.44) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Added mandatory multilingual local_names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi), saturated_fat_g estimate from USDA SR Legacy, GI analogous estimate with University of Sydney basis, full ontology with FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors, all three required health conditions, comprehensive aliases, LLM description exceeding 200 characters.
Iteration 2: Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving calculation to 3.3. Documented all -1 fields in notes with reason and source consulted.

Iteration 3 (current): Fixed g3 — expanded notes to individually document each group of -1 fields (fatty acids, vitamins, micronutrients, water) with specific reason and source consulted per field group. Fixed g5 — corrected foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to comply with allowed enum values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
